name: Deposition Preparation
summary: This skill guides an LLM to produce a comprehensive deposition preparation package for taking or defending depositions in U.S. civil litigation, anchored in FRCP 26 and 30 with supporting guidance from FRE 612, 613, 702, 803, and 901. It features mandatory pre-draft and post-draft alignment checkpoints, a required issue-mapping table tied to legal elements, time allocation, exhibit integration guide, impeachment materials table using the commit-credit-confront method, and witness-type adaptations for party, fact, expert, 30(b)(6), and apex witnesses. The skill delivers outputs in three modes (Full Prep Package, At-the-Table condensed outline, and Defending Pack) with built-in anti-hallucination guardrails and explicit professional responsibility compliance.
content:
# Deposition Preparation: Strategic Planning and Execution

## 1. Opening Context

Deposition preparation is the cornerstone of modern civil litigation. In a legal system where fewer than one percent of cases reach a jury, the deposition is often the functional equivalent of trial. A well-executed deposition can secure the admissions necessary to prevail on a motion for summary judgment, destroy an expert's credibility, or lock a party into a narrative that cannot be escaped at trial. Conversely, poor preparation leads to missed opportunities, the inadvertent disclosure of privileged information, and the creation of a record that serves the opponent's narrative rather than the client's.

This skill produces a deposition preparation package that a litigation team can actually use at the table: a strategic plan tied to the elements and defenses, a defensible topic-and-time allocation, a witness-specific outline with exhibit and impeachment integration, and (where defending) an ethics-safe witness prep agenda that anticipates opposing counsel's goals. Depositions are one of the highest-leverage moments in U.S. civil litigation because testimony becomes a permanent evidentiary artifact; it drives summary judgment, settlement posture, expert framing, trial examination, and impeachment. When deposition prep is done poorly, the record is incomplete, admissions are missed, privilege is waived, sanctions risk increases, and the client can be locked into avoidable testimony that cannot be rehabilitated.

Professional standards governing this work product include the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure (especially FRCP 26 and FRCP 30), court orders (scheduling orders, protective orders, ESI/protocol orders), evidence rules that affect later admissibility and impeachment (FRE 612, 613, 702, 803, and 901), and ethical constraints on witness preparation, confidentiality, and candor. This skill is designed to operate with imperfect information while surfacing assumptions and requiring attorney confirmation at defined checkpoints.

## 2. Understanding the Context and Purpose

A deposition preparation package exists to convert case theory into a controlled, sequenced examination plan that produces usable testimony under the constraints of the forum's discovery rules and the practical realities of a live witness. In the matter lifecycle, depositions typically occur after initial pleadings and meaningful written discovery (or disclosures), but often before dispositive motion practice and expert depositions. The core strategic function differs depending on posture.

The deposition serves two primary, often competing, purposes: discovery (finding out what the witness knows) and preservation (locking the witness into testimony for use at trial or in motion practice). An attorney must decide which purpose predominates before drafting a single question. In a discovery-heavy deposition, the goal is to be expansive, using open-ended questions to explore the boundaries of the witness's knowledge. In a preservation or "trial" deposition, the goal is control—using leading questions to build a clean record of admissions.

When taking a deposition, the package must help the examining attorney (i) learn what the witness knows and what they will claim not to know, (ii) lock the witness into a coherent story with commit points, (iii) secure admissions that map cleanly onto elements, defenses, and damages models, (iv) create impeachment materials by establishing foundation for documents and prior statements, and (v) obtain follow-up discovery targets. When defending a deposition, the package must help counsel (i) reduce testimony risk, (ii) preserve privilege and work-product, (iii) avoid volunteering and narrative drift, (iv) anticipate exhibit traps and "gotcha" sequences, and (v) protect the client's credibility while leaving room for later explanation.

The intended readers and users are the examining or defending attorney at the deposition, the broader trial team using testimony for motions and trial, and sometimes the client or in-house counsel who needs a clear view of risk, goals, and follow-up items. They expect concrete outputs: prioritized objectives, a usable outline with question sequences that match those objectives, exhibit handling scripts, and a record-building plan that is procedurally sound. The ultimate audience for the resulting transcript is often not the witness, but a judge deciding a Rule 56 motion or a jury evaluating credibility through impeachment at trial.

This skill covers preparation to take or defend depositions in U.S. civil litigation, with primary grounding in federal practice. It does not replace jurisdiction-specific motion practice regarding deposition limits, protective orders, or discovery sanctions; instead, it prompts verification and adaptation when local rules, state rules, or court orders diverge.

## 3. Prerequisites and Information Gathering

Before drafting, the LLM should obtain enough information to avoid silent mismatches—wrong witness, wrong posture, wrong time limits, wrong objectives—while still moving forward. The guiding principle is to never block progress waiting for perfect inputs, but also to never silently assume key parameters. If required information is missing and the user declines to provide it, the LLM must proceed using clearly labeled assumptions and list "Open Items / Needed Inputs" for attorney follow-up.

### Checkpoint A: Pre-Draft Intake Snapshot (Mandatory)

Checkpoint A must be asked every time unless the user explicitly says "use defaults" or "just draft." The LLM should request: (a) the deponent's identity and witness type (party, nonparty fact witness, expert, corporate representative under FRCP 30(b)(6), or "apex" executive); (b) whether counsel is taking or defending and which side is represented; (c) the top two or three ranked objectives for the deposition, selected from discovery, summary judgment admissions, trial preservation, impeachment setup, credibility assessment, damages exploration, or expert reliability challenge; and (d) the controlling time limit, including whether a court order or stipulation modifies the default. For federal civil cases, the default time limit is one day of seven hours unless otherwise stipulated or ordered under FRCP 30(d)(1).

A high-quality intake snapshot also asks one "case posture" question that prevents strategic misfires: "What is the next major milestone—summary judgment, mediation, class certification, Daubert, preliminary injunction, or trial?" This tunes admissions and sequencing; for example, if summary judgment is the next milestone, the outline should emphasize element-by-element commit points rather than open-ended exploratory lines.

If the user does not respond, the LLM should apply and clearly label these defaults: taking deposition (unless context strongly indicates defending); objectives of discovery and impeachment setup; seven hours; hybrid topical-chronological outline format.

### Documents and Record Materials

Documents and record materials that substantially improve quality include: the deposition notice (including topics for a 30(b)(6) deposition); the operative complaint with exhibits and the operative answer with affirmative defenses; initial disclosures under FRCP 26(a)(1) and any supplementation history under FRCP 26(e); key written discovery responses (interrogatories, requests for production, requests for admission); key documents the witness authored, received, or that bear on the targeted issues (the "hot doc" set); any declarations, affidavits, or prior testimony by the witness; expert reports and disclosures if the witness is an expert under FRCP 26(a)(2); the scheduling order and any protective order; and meet-and-confer correspondence concerning scope disputes.

For a FRCP 30(b)(6) deposition, the LLM must request the notice topic list and identify the designated witness(es), because the outline must be structured around topics and the "reasonable particularity" requirement for the notice shapes both scope and objections. For defending a deposition, the LLM should also request any subpoenas, document requests accompanying the notice, and any known exhibits opposing counsel has previewed.

If any of these materials are missing, the LLM must explicitly flag them for the user and ask for the specific missing materials before finalizing the strategic mapping. Do not stall, but do not pretend completeness either.

## 4. Step-by-Step Process

### Step 1: Build the Case Theory Spine and Issue Map

Deposition outlines fail when they are topic lists untethered to burdens and elements. Before drafting questions, every substantive topic in the outline must serve a purpose: establishing a prima facie element, supporting an affirmative defense, or attacking credibility.

The LLM should start by extracting or inferring the claims, defenses, and key disputed issues, including burdens of proof and any shifting burdens (for example, affirmative defenses, statutory exemptions, or damages mitigation). If pleadings are available, read them and translate into an issue-element table. If pleadings are not available, create a provisional issue map with clear "[ASSUMED]" markers and ask the user to confirm.

In federal court, the discovery scope is defined by relevance and proportionality under FRCP 26(b)(1). The issue map is how you ensure proportionality: you can justify time spent by showing its link to claims and defenses and the importance of the issues. When defending, the same map is used to identify sensitive areas where the witness is likely to be pushed into speculation or privileged territory.

The required deliverable is an Issue Mapping Table with five columns: "Issue/Element," "What We Need to Prove/Disprove," "What This Witness Can Establish," "Best Documents/Prior Statements," and "Target Admission/Lock Point." For example, in a negligence case, if "notice" is an element, the lock point might be: "You received the inspection report (Ex. 12) on March 3 and understood it described the hazard." If the deponent is a witness to a slip-and-fall, the objective is not just "what did you see" but "establish the witness's lack of knowledge regarding the duration of the spill to defeat constructive notice." The LLM should not invent evidence; if the record is unknown, it should use placeholders and flag them for attorney verification.

Common mistakes include mapping to generalized themes ("carelessness," "bad faith") rather than legally cognizable elements, or failing to separate story-building from record-building. Opposing counsel will challenge relevance objections or seek protective orders if the outline appears untethered to the issues.

### Step 2: Profile the Witness and Choose the Examination Architecture

The outline structure should match witness type and the litigation objective. A pure chronology is often best for percipient witnesses who experienced events in sequence and where credibility is central. A topical structure is often best for corporate representatives (because the notice topics control scope) and for experts (because opinions and bases are modular). A hybrid structure is frequently best for party witnesses: start with background and role, then a short chronology to anchor the narrative, then issue-driven topical modules.

The LLM should compile a witness profile that includes role and tenure; relationship to key actors; likely motivation or bias; prior statements (written statements, interviews, declarations, emails, texts); prior testimony (depositions, hearings, arbitration testimony); and the critical distinction between personal knowledge, hearsay, and learned knowledge (especially for 30(b)(6) designees). The LLM should also identify all documents authored by, received by, or mentioning the witness, including metadata sources such as system logs, training records, and audit trails.

The LLM should include an "anticipated demeanor and risk" paragraph that flags whether the witness is likely to be evasive, overconfident, anxious, technical, or combative, and how that affects questioning style. With an overconfident witness, you may accelerate commit points and use documents to tighten. With an anxious witness, you may slow down and avoid compound questions. With an evasive witness, plan for repeated "I don't recall" responses and prepare document-based memory refreshment sequences under FRE 612.

Common mistakes include using the same outline architecture for every witness, failing to plan for "I don't recall" patterns, or ignoring the difference between personal knowledge and corporate knowledge. Opposing counsel will exploit poor structure by running out the clock, forcing disorganized follow-ups, or objecting on form to break questioning momentum.

### Step 3: Draft the Deposition Outline as a Record-Building Instrument

The outline should be drafted as a record-building instrument, not just a question list. It must be easy to execute under time pressure and easy for later users (summary judgment drafter, trial examiner) to mine for usable testimony. The LLM should produce an outline containing: objectives (ranked); time allocation; a preliminary/admonitions module; background/foundation tailored to witness type; substantive modules each containing connection-to-issues, exhibits, funnel sequence, commit points, and boxing-in; and a closing completeness module.

The time allocation is a required deliverable: a table listing each topic, estimated time, priority (1–5), and notes, with the total approximating the controlling limit. For federal court, the presumptive limit is seven hours under FRCP 30(d)(1). The LLM should note that whether breaks count against examination time varies by stipulation, court order, and practice; this must be confirmed with the attorney for the specific case.

The preliminary section (approximately 15 minutes) should include the functional admonitions that protect the record: verbal responses required, no head nods, ask for clarification if a question is unclear, correct misunderstandings, and break rules. It should also include the preparation inquiry: when the witness learned of the deposition, who they met with to prepare, what documents they reviewed, and whether any outlines or memoranda were used in preparation. When defending, the preliminary section should also include a short privilege and instruction-not-to-answer reminder: under FRCP 30(c)(2), a person may be instructed not to answer only when necessary to preserve a privilege, to enforce a limitation ordered by the court, or to present a motion under FRCP 30(d)(3). That rule is often mishandled; the outline should be drafted to avoid creating a sanctionable instruction pattern.

For each substantive module, the LLM should write questions using a predictable funnel technique. This is the most important tactical discipline in deposition practice. Start with open narrative questions ("Tell me everything you remember about the meeting on June 1st") to gather information before narrowing the witness's room to maneuver. Then move to investigative follow-up ("Who sat where? What documents were on the table? Who spoke first?"). Then conduct document examination: authenticate the document ("Is this an email you received?"), have the witness read the relevant passage, ask for their interpretation at the time ("What did you understand this to mean when you received it?"), and lock in the commitment. Then close with the "Is That All" exhaustion phase ("Besides what you just told me, is there anything else? Are you sure?"), followed by the confirming lock ("So it's fair to say that Mr. Jones never mentioned the budget deficit during that meeting, correct?").

A common and costly mistake is jumping to the locking-in phase too early. If you lead the witness immediately, you suggest the answer, which may prevent you from discovering a more damaging fact the witness would have volunteered in an open-ended format.

Each substantive module must also include a "completeness box" at the end—what the original skill calls the FWD (Fact/Witness/Document) Boxing-In technique. This is the most critical tool for summary judgment preparation. You must exhaust the witness's memory and knowledge so they cannot "remember" new facts at trial that contradict your motion. After a witness provides a list of reasons, people, or events, ask: "Is that the complete list of everyone who was present?" "Have you now told me every reason why you terminated the plaintiff?" "Other than what we just covered, is there anything else you rely on for your understanding of this topic?" "If we go to trial six months from now, will you have any other reasons to add to this list?" Failing to ask the catch-all question is one of the most common errors in deposition practice; without it, the witness can show up at trial with a "refreshed memory" and your impeachment will be significantly weakened.

The conclusion section (approximately 15 minutes) should include a completeness check, identification of additional witnesses or documents, confirmation of truthfulness and ability to testify, and reservation of rights or continuation if needed.

### Step 4: Integrate Exhibits and Impeachment Materials

A deposition plan is only as good as its exhibits strategy. The LLM should create two required deliverables: an Exhibit Integration Guide and an Impeachment Materials Table.

The Exhibit Integration Guide should be a table specifying: Topic, Exhibit, When to Use, Purpose, Foundation Questions, and Impeachment Hook. For every major exhibit, the LLM should prepare a Foundation Script. An illustrative example: "I'm handing you what's been marked as Exhibit 4. Do you recognize it? It's an email from you to Sarah Smith dated January 10, correct? You wrote this in the ordinary course of your job, right? Looking at the second paragraph, you wrote [quote]. What did you mean by that?" Ensure the outline includes the necessary questions to establish authentication under FRE 901 and, where the witness is the custodian or a knowledgeable participant, the business records exception under FRE 803(6).

The Impeachment Materials Table should specify: Topic, Prior Statement, Source/Citation, Expected Current Testimony, Contradiction, and Approach. When you know a witness has contradicted a prior statement (an email, a declaration, prior testimony), do not confront them immediately. Follow the "Commit, Credit, Confront" method. First, commit: get them to state the inconsistent position clearly ("You never saw the warning sign, correct?"). Second, credit: build up the reliability of the contradicting document ("You signed this declaration under penalty of perjury two weeks after the accident, right? Your memory was fresh then? You were being truthful?"). Third, confront: present the contradiction ("Then why does your declaration say, 'I saw the yellow warning sign and walked around it'?"). If exact citations (page, line, paragraph) are not available, insert placeholders and mark them for attorney verification. A savvy defender will object to "mischaracterizing the record," so the impeachment plan should reference the exact page, line, or paragraph of the prior statement.

If the case is in federal court and a document is used to refresh recollection during testimony, the LLM should flag FRE 612 implications: the adverse party may be entitled to production of the writing used to refresh. For prior inconsistent statements, the LLM should flag the requirements and tactical considerations under FRE 613, including when you must show or disclose the statement to the witness and how to preserve the opportunity to explain or deny. Where state evidence rules differ from the federal rules on these points, the LLM should mark the issue for attorney verification rather than asserting a specific state standard.

The LLM should also prompt for non-traditional exhibits that often prove decisive: calendars, metadata, system logs, training records, ticketing systems, version histories, and collaboration platform records. Many witnesses can talk around a narrative document, but logs and metadata force precision about timing and access.

Common mistakes include bringing too many exhibits with no plan, failing to sequence exhibits so that admissions build on one another, and confronting too early (triggering defensiveness and "refreshed" memory). Opposing counsel will attempt to prevent clean exhibit use by objecting to foundation, coaching the witness to "not interpret," or creating confusion about what the document shows. Your plan should therefore separate authentication questions from interpretation questions.

### Step 5: Adapt the Package for Posture and Witness Type

The preparation package must be adapted based on whether counsel is taking or defending, and based on the specific witness type.

**Defending Depositions.** When defending, the deposition package is fundamentally a risk-management tool. The LLM should produce: an anticipated topics and exhibits section predicting the adversary's likely path based on pleadings and produced documents; a vulnerabilities and safe themes section helping the witness stay within personal knowledge and avoid speculation; an ethics-safe prep session agenda including review of key documents, review of prior statements, a walk-through of the likely question sequence, and practice on answering concisely; an objection and privilege framework with specific reminders tied to FRCP 30(c)(2); and rehabilitation topics for re-examination. The agenda must include behavioral guidance (listen to the full question, pause before answering, answer only the question asked, understand the distinction between "I don't recall" and "I don't know"), and it must explicitly prohibit coaching false testimony.

In federal court, speaking objections—those that suggest an answer to the witness, such as "Objection, he already answered that, he's saying he doesn't know"—are prohibited under FRCP 30(c)(2). The objecting attorney may only state the legal basis concisely (for example, "Objection, form"). Some state courts, such as New York under Part 221 of the Uniform Rules for the Conduct of Depositions, have even more restrictive provisions. The LLM should verify the objection practice rules for the specific forum.

**FRCP 30(b)(6) Corporate Representatives.** The outline must be structured around the noticed topics, and the LLM should include questions that test preparation adequacy: "Who did you speak with to prepare?" "What repositories did you search?" "What policies did you review?" FRCP 30(b)(6) requires the organization to designate one or more persons to testify about information known or reasonably available to the organization. Inadequate preparation can support a motion to compel or additional relief depending on the court's practice; the LLM should flag the specific consequences as requiring attorney verification for the forum. The outline must also include "scope fences" so defending counsel can object that questions go beyond the noticed topics when appropriate, while recognizing that many courts interpret topics with reasonable flexibility. When taking a 30(b)(6), include bind-the-corporation admissions strategy: because the designee speaks for the organization, admissions are party-opponent statements.

**Expert Witnesses.** The outline should lock in: opinions stated in the report; bases and data considered; methodology and its application; and concessions helpful to motions in limine or reliability challenges. In federal court, the admissibility framework for expert testimony is anchored in FRE 702, which was amended effective December 1, 2023, to clarify that the proponent must establish admissibility by a preponderance of the evidence and that reliability requirements apply to both the expert's opinion and its application to the facts. The LLM should closely track the rule text from a verifiable source rather than paraphrasing aggressively, and should flag any interpretive gloss for attorney verification. A good expert module includes concrete concession targets: assumptions not tested, alternative causes not ruled out, missing data, litigation-driven selection bias, compensation and repeat-player patterns, prior exclusions by other courts, and limits on generalization. In some state courts, the governing expert admissibility standard may differ (for example, jurisdictions following Frye rather than Daubert); the LLM should not state which standard applies without jurisdiction confirmation.

**Apex Witnesses.** Some jurisdictions impose heightened scrutiny or require a showing of unique personal knowledge before deposing high-level executives. Because the apex doctrine is not uniform, the LLM should ask for the jurisdiction and whether a protective order motion is contemplated, then tailor preparation to either justify the deposition (if taking) or support limitation or protective relief (if defending). The LLM should treat any apex doctrine statements as jurisdiction-dependent and flag them for attorney verification.

### Step 6: Produce the Deliverables in Two Lanes and Clearly Mark Assumptions

The LLM should output a two-lane deliverable by default. Lane 1 is the Full Deposition Prep Package: strategy overview tied to the case milestone, issue-element map table, deposition outline with time allocation, exhibit integration guide, impeachment materials table, and follow-up discovery targets. Lane 2 is the condensed At-the-Table One-Pager: top objectives, three must-get admissions, exhibit order, and a time-topic clock with fallback paths if time runs short. A third output mode, the Defending Pack, should be produced when counsel is defending: anticipated topics, vulnerabilities, prep session agenda, and rehabilitation plan.

At the very top of the output, before any substantive content, the LLM must include two mandatory front-matter sections. First, "Assumptions Used," listing at minimum: taking or defending posture; time limit; objectives; witness type; and key case facts relied on. Second, "Open Items / Needed Inputs," identifying missing pleadings, discovery, exhibits, and any jurisdictional rule uncertainties. This front matter is a critical anti-hallucination guardrail: it tells the attorney exactly what was assumed and what must be verified.

A common mistake is delivering a long outline that is unusable live. The one-pager is not optional; it is the execution instrument. Opposing counsel benefits when the examining attorney cannot find the next move; the condensed lane prevents that.

### Step 7: Post-Draft Alignment and Iteration (Checkpoint B)

Checkpoint B is equally important as Checkpoint A because deposition prep is iterative: once the attorney sees the outline and exhibit plan, they realize what they actually need at the table. After delivering the initial package, the LLM must ask: Does this match the right witness and posture (taking or defending)? Which add-on modules should be generated next: a condensed one-page at-the-table outline, verbatim question sequences for three key admissions, an exhibit pack with authentication scripts, impeachment scripts using the commit-credit-confront method, a defending prep agenda, or a topic deep-dive for a specific area? Are there any "red-line topics" to avoid or handle delicately? What are the three must-get admissions (or does the attorney confirm the three proposed)?

If the user does not answer, the LLM should recommend the next best refinement based on stated objectives and proceed if authorized.

## 5. Critical Legal Standards and Compliance

This skill's outputs are used in adversarial settings and must comply with procedural rules, evidence rules, and professional responsibility obligations. All output requires attorney review before use and does not constitute legal advice.

**Governing Procedural Rules.** FRCP 30 governs depositions by oral examination in federal court, including the seven-hour presumptive time limit under FRCP 30(d)(1), the objection and instruction-not-to-answer standards under FRCP 30(c)(2), and the sanctions and protective order mechanisms under FRCP 30(d)(2) and (3). FRCP 26 governs discovery scope, proportionality under FRCP 26(b)(1), required disclosures under FRCP 26(a), and expert disclosure requirements under FRCP 26(a)(2). FRCP 32 governs the use of depositions in court proceedings, including admissibility standards that vary by context; the LLM should flag specific FRCP 32 applications for attorney verification.

**Evidence Rules for Exhibits and Impeachment.** FRE 612 governs writings used to refresh a witness's memory, with potential production obligations. FRE 613 governs the use of prior inconsistent statements for impeachment, including disclosure requirements. FRE 702 (as amended December 1, 2023) governs expert testimony admissibility. FRE 803(6) provides the business records hearsay exception relevant to document foundation. FRE 901 governs authentication requirements.

**Ethics of Witness Preparation.** ABA Formal Opinion 508 addresses the ethics of witness preparation. An attorney may discuss the case and the evidence with the witness, review documents, and walk through likely questions, but cannot coach the witness to provide false testimony. Model Rule 3.3 (candor toward the tribunal) requires correction of known false testimony. Model Rule 3.4 (fairness to opposing party and counsel) prohibits obstructing access to evidence and using methods of obtaining evidence that violate the rights of third parties. Model Rule 1.1 requires competent representation, including understanding of procedural rules and evidentiary consequences. Model Rule 1.6 requires protection of information relating to the representation. Model Rule 5.3 addresses responsibilities regarding nonlawyer assistants. Because individual jurisdictions adopt and may modify the Model Rules, the LLM should verify the specific text and rule number in the governing jurisdiction.

**Attorney-Client Privilege.** When defending, counsel must be vigilant about Upjohn warnings in the corporate context, where the attorney-client privilege extends to communications between corporate counsel and employees at all levels when made for the purpose of obtaining legal advice. If a question asks for the contents of a conversation with counsel, the defending attorney must object and instruct the witness not to answer to preserve the privilege under FRCP 30(c)(2). The LLM should remind that communications with counsel and deposition preparation materials can be discoverable in narrow circumstances (for example, when used to refresh under FRE 612 or where waiver occurs), and therefore any "what did you review" preparation questions must be planned with privilege strategy in mind.

**Anti-Hallucination Rule for Authorities.** The LLM may cite FRCP and FRE using Cornell LII URLs. For any additional legal authorities—case law, state rules, local rules, ethics opinions—the LLM must either provide a verifiable URL from an official or reputable source or mark the citation for attorney verification. Under no circumstances should the LLM generate invented case citations or rule references. If the skill produces an outline that includes "expected answers," those must be explicitly labeled as hypotheses and must never be presented as facts.

**Malpractice Risk Areas.** The highest-risk areas in deposition work include: misstating the governing deposition limit; advising improper instructions not to answer that trigger sanctions; missing key element admissions because the outline is untethered to the issues; eliciting harmful testimony through compound or sloppy questions; waiving privilege through careless preparation inquiries; failing to preserve correction mechanisms; and failing to object to correctable form errors, which may waive the objection at trial. Mitigation strategies include tying every major line to the issue map, using the time allocation, keeping questions single-fact, planning exhibit sequencing in advance, and flagging privilege and correction rules for attorney review.

## 6. Quality Control and Review

Before finalizing the deposition package, the LLM must perform a comprehensive strategic audit.

First, confirm the governing procedural framework: the forum (federal versus state), any deposition protocol order, and the actual time limits and examination allocations. For federal cases, verify that the outline reflects FRCP 30's limits and objection/instruction standards and that scope aligns with FRCP 26(b)(1) proportionality.

Second, audit factual assertions. Every assertion about "what happened" must be traceable to a document, a pleading allegation, a discovery response, or an attorney-provided fact summary. If the LLM had to assume facts, those must be marked as assumptions and not embedded as truths in questions. A leading question that assumes a disputed fact can educate the witness and distort the record.

Third, evaluate usability. Does each substantive module contain a clear objective, the key exhibits, and at least two commit points? Does the time allocation match the objectives, or is time wasted on background? Can the examining attorney execute the plan with a clean sequence even if the witness is evasive?

Fourth, check evidentiary and impeachment readiness. Are the foundation scripts adequate to authenticate documents under FRE 901? Are prior statements tied to an impeachment approach that complies with FRE 613? Are refresh plans sensitive to FRE 612 production risks?

Fifth, verify the admission tracker: does every claim and defense in the pleadings have a corresponding section in the outline designed to elicit a necessary admission?

Sixth, confirm that every major topic ends with an exhaustion and boxing-in question.

Seventh, review authority integrity. Any citations beyond FRCP and FRE must be verified via web search and flagged if not verified. Include a citation verification note: "All non-rule authorities require attorney verification; no case citations were generated unless sourced by URL."

Finally, ensure the output flags uncertainties and prompts attorney decisions: whether to seek additional time, whether to stipulate to exhibit handling, whether to pursue a protective order, whether to reserve certain topics for another witness, and whether to bring a motion under FRCP 30(d)(3) if conduct becomes obstructive.

The complete self-audit checklist should confirm: objectives ranked and tied to claims, defenses, and issues; issue mapping table completed; time allocations provided and total matches time limit; funnel technique present in each substantive module; FWD boxing-in included in each module; exhibit integration guide present; impeachment table present (even if with placeholders); defending-mode materials included if defending; and assumptions and open items listed prominently.

## 7. Jurisdiction-Specific Adaptations

This skill is written for U.S. litigation with federal rules as the default backbone. Adaptation is required in three main areas: time limits, objection practice, and special doctrines.

In U.S. federal court, the default deposition duration is one day of seven hours under FRCP 30(d)(1) and objection and instruction practice is governed by FRCP 30(c)(2). Many state systems differ materially. California generally limits depositions to seven hours of total testimony time, with exceptions for experts and complex cases under CCP § 2025.290, and has specific notice-of-video-recording requirements. Texas uses a "Level" system (Level 1, 2, or 3) that dictates the total hours allowed for all depositions in a case under Rule 190. New York follows Part 221 of the Uniform Rules for the Conduct of Depositions, which strictly prohibits speaking objections and suggestive interruptions. Because state-by-state variation is significant, the LLM must ask the user to identify the state and, if available, provide the state rule or local rule citation; if not provided, the LLM should proceed using federal defaults but label them as assumptions.

Evidence rules for refresh and impeachment frequently mirror the Federal Rules of Evidence, but not always. If the matter is in state court, the LLM should ask whether the jurisdiction follows FRE analogs for Rules 612 and 613; if unknown, it should flag the issue for attorney verification and avoid making definitive statements.

Expert deposition strategy in federal court should reflect FRE 702 (including the 2023 amendment). In state courts, the governing expert admissibility standard may differ, particularly in jurisdictions following the Frye general-acceptance test rather than the Daubert reliability framework. The LLM should not state which standard applies without jurisdiction confirmation and a verifiable source.

Apex deposition limitations and corporate representative practice also vary by jurisdiction. The LLM should treat any apex doctrine statements as jurisdiction-dependent and request the controlling jurisdiction and any relevant protective order precedent or local practice notes; absent that, it should provide only general risk framing and flag the issue for attorney verification.

---

## Mandatory Guardrail on Authorities

The LLM may cite FRCP and FRE using Cornell LII URLs (https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frcp and https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/fre). For any additional legal authorities (case law, state rules, local rules, ethics opinions), the LLM must either provide a verifiable URL or label the authority for attorney verification. The LLM must never generate invented citations. All deposition preparation outputs must be reviewed by a licensed attorney responsible for the matter before use.
